---
pagetitle: "jstats — Reference"
title-prefix: ""
---

## Reference

Below are the jstats functions and datasets, grouped by what they do. First, two
quick notes on getting help.

### Getting help within RStudio

Every function has a built-in help page. The quick way to open one in RStudio is to
click the function's name — in your script or the console — and press **F1** (or
**Fn+F1** on most Macs). In any R setup you can also type `?` followed by the name —
for example, `?jfreq`. Either way you get the description, the options, and worked
examples.

### What this site adds

The function and dataset names below link to those same help pages online — and those
pages rebuild themselves automatically from the package. Whenever the package changes
or a new function is added, they regenerate from its built-in documentation and the
examples are re-run, so the documentation and the code never drift apart: what you see
is always current. The payoff is that every example shows the real output the call
produces, not output typed in by hand — so you can copy one and trust it matches what
you'll get.

::: {.callout-note title="Growing into a fuller reference"}
Over time this section will grow into a task-indexed reference — a place to look up
how to do a specific thing while you work, organized by what you want to do rather
than by function name. For now, it's a grouped map of what's available, with each
name linking to its full help page.
:::

### Describe and explore

- [`jdesc`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jdesc.html) — descriptive statistics for one or more variables.
- [`jfreq`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jfreq.html) — frequency tables for categorical variables.
- [`jscreen`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jscreen.html) — a one-call overview of a whole dataset: ranges, missing values, possible outliers, and what kind each variable looks to be.

### Tests and models

- [`jt`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jt.html) — independent-samples or paired-samples t-test.
- [`jaov`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jaov.html) — one-way ANOVA.
- [`jcrosstab`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jcrosstab.html) — cross-tabulation, with an optional chi-square test.
- [`jcorr`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jcorr.html) — a correlation matrix, with p-values and the N behind each pair.
- [`jlm`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jlm.html) — linear regression.
- [`jlogistic`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jlogistic.html) — logistic regression.

### Scales and reliability

- [`jalpha`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jalpha.html) — Cronbach's alpha reliability analysis for a set of scale items.
- [`jsum`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jsum.html) — a row-wise sum across several variables (for example, a scale total).
- [`javg`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/javg.html) — a row-wise mean across several variables (for example, a scale score).

### Prepare and manage your data

- [`jrecode`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jrecode.html) — recode a variable using an explicit value mapping, with optional labels.
- [`jrelabel`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jrelabel.html) — add or change a variable's variable- and value-labels.
- [`jdeclare_udm`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jdeclare_udm.html) — declare user-defined missing values (values that should count as missing, such as a "Refused" code) without overwriting your data.
- [`jsubset`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jsubset.html) — set a case-selection filter so functions run on a chosen subset of cases.
- [`jcomplete`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jcomplete.html) — set a listwise complete-case filter so a run of analyses share a matching N.
- [`jcopy`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jcopy.html) — copy a data frame, carrying any of its variable settings along with it.

### Open, save, and convert files

- [`jload`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jload.html) — open a data file (from commercial statistical software, Excel, or CSV) in one command, bringing its value labels, variable labels, and missing-value codes along automatically.
- [`jsave`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jsave.html) — save a data frame back out to a file.
- [`jconvert`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jconvert.html) — convert missing-value declarations between the conventions different programs use.
- [`jdata_dir`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jdata_dir.html) — show the folder jstats reads your data files from.

### Set up your session

- [`juse`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/juse.html) — set (or display) the default dataset, so you don't have to name it in every call.
- [`joptions`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/joptions.html) — set or display session-level options, such as the data folder.
- [`joutput`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/joutput.html) — set how much detail jstats prints (the output verbosity level).
- [`jupdate`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jupdate.html) — update jstats to the latest version (one command; restart R afterward to load it).

### Plots

- [`jplot`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jplot.html) — plot a jstats result, or draw a quick plot of variables straight from a dataset.

### Fine-tuning: tell jstats how to treat a variable

Most users will not need these. jstats works out what kind each variable is on
its own; these let you override that when you want a particular variable handled
a particular way.

- [`jnumeric`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jnumeric.html) — treat a variable as numeric.
- [`jdummy`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jdummy.html) — treat a categorical variable as a set of dummy variables in regression.
- [`jcount`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jcount.html) — treat a variable as a count.
- [`jlikert`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/jlikert.html) — treat a variable as an ordered (Likert) response item.

### Datasets

jstats ships with two practice datasets, both available the moment the package loads:

- [`community`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/community.html) — a clean 100-respondent community survey (income, education, age, a wellbeing score, several yes/no items, a region, and a five-item attitude scale). The package's default example data; it's what every function's help examples use.
- [`clinic`](https://jma61.github.io/jstats/reference/clinic.html) — a deliberately messy 70-client mental-health and wellbeing sample, the companion for practicing data cleaning: it carries undeclared missing-value codes, stripped labels, and imperfect scale items to work through.

### On the way

A few capabilities are planned for future versions — among them regression for
count outcomes (Poisson and negative-binomial models) and exporting formatted,
APA-style results to Word. The function set above is what ships today.
